summary:
Ancient Greek and Roman authors attributed the destruction of [[event:57|Mycenaean Greece]] in part to an invasion by Dorian-speaking Greeks from northwestern Greece, who supposedly displaced the earlier Achaean population and ushered in the Greek Dark Ages. This tradition, known as the Return of the Heraclidae, described the Dorians as descendants of Heracles reclaiming ancestral lands. Modern archaeology has complicated this picture considerably: the destruction levels at Mycenaean palace sites around 1200 BC show no consistent signs of a single invading group, and the simultaneous collapse of Bronze Age systems across the eastern Mediterranean suggests multiple contributing factors.

Nevertheless, Greek dialect geography supports some major population movement during the period. Dorian dialects came to dominate the Peloponnese, Crete, and parts of Asia Minor in historical times, replacing or overlaying the dialects associated with Mycenaean civilization. Sparta, Corinth, and Argos all became Dorian cities in the historical period, and Dorian identity remained a significant cultural and political marker well into the classical age.

The [[event:1826|Greek Dark Ages]] that followed the collapse saw a dramatic reduction in population, abandonment of writing, and contraction of long-distance trade. Whether the arrival of Dorian-speaking groups was a cause, a consequence, or merely a contemporary development of the broader [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]] remains debated. What is clear is that Greek society emerged from this period fundamentally transformed, with the palace-centered economy replaced by smaller, more dispersed Iron Age communities.
